rootCauseAnalysis.summary: A NetworkPolicy 'default-network-policy' with empty podSelector and Ingress policyType but no ingress rules blocks ALL ingress traffic to every pod in demo-frontend, causing the traffic-gen readiness probe to fail when it tries to reach web-frontend:8080/healthz.
causalChain: Signal: KubeDeploymentReplicasMismatch — Deployment 'traffic-gen' has 1 desired replica but 0 available (ready)
Why? Pod 'traffic-gen-77f9ffc59c-22cfs' is Running but its readiness probe fails persistently (25 Unhealthy events)
Why? The readiness probe 'curl -sf http://web-frontend.demo-frontend:8080/healthz' cannot reach web-frontend — returns failure
Why? NetworkPolicy 'default-network-policy' (podSelector: {}, policyTypes: [Ingress], no ingress rules) blocks ALL ingress traffic to all pods in demo-frontend, including web-frontend pods

Root cause: NetworkPolicy 'default-network-policy' implements a deny-all-ingress policy for the entire demo-frontend namespace, injected by kubernaut-demo infrastructure, with no ingress allowance rules defined

dueDiligence.targetAccuracy: Targeting NetworkPolicy/default-network-policy because it is the resource whose configuration (or deletion) directly fixes the problem. The Deployment and Pod are correctly configured — they are victims of the network policy. Adding ingress rules or deleting the NetworkPolicy would restore pod-to-pod connectivity and allow the readiness probe to pass.
dueDiligence.proportionality: The NetworkPolicy applies to the entire demo-frontend namespace (empty podSelector), affecting all pods. However, the remediation target is precisely the NetworkPolicy resource — a single, well-defined configuration change (add ingress rules or delete the policy) resolves the issue. This is appropriately scoped.
dueDiligence.scopeCompleteness: Checked: Deployment spec, ReplicaSet, Pod status/events/logs, NetworkPolicy (all policies in namespace), node assignment. Did not retrieve the Service object (Knative API ambiguity prevented it), but this does not affect the conclusion since the NetworkPolicy blocks traffic before it reaches the service. No other NetworkPolicies exist in the namespace.
dueDiligence.causalCompleteness: Traced fully: signal → pod not ready → readiness probe failure → network unreachable → NetworkPolicy deny-all-ingress. The NetworkPolicy spec was verified directly: empty podSelector + Ingress policyType + no ingress rules = deny-all. Cannot go deeper; the NetworkPolicy is the root configuration causing the block.
dueDiligence.evidenceSufficiency: All claims are backed by tool output: (1) Pod events show 25 Unhealthy readiness probe failures; (2) Pod logs show sustained probe=failure after initial successes; (3) NetworkPolicy spec confirmed: podSelector:{}, policyTypes:[Ingress], no ingress rules; (4) NetworkPolicy creationTimestamp 11:31:54Z correlates with probe failures starting at 11:31:58Z. Service object could not be retrieved (ambiguous API group with Knative), but web-frontend pods are confirmed Ready via pod listing.
dueDiligence.regressionAwareness: No prior remediation history exists for NetworkPolicy/default-network-policy in demo-frontend (confirmed via get_namespaced_resource_context). N/A — no previously failed remediations to avoid.
dueDiligence.alternativeHypotheses: Considered: (1) web-frontend service misconfiguration — ruled out: web-frontend pods are Ready and responding to kubelet probes; (2) DNS resolution failure — ruled out: pod logs show initial probe=success proving DNS and connectivity worked before the NetworkPolicy was applied; (3) Resource limits/OOMKill — ruled out: 0 restarts, container is Running; (4) Node network issues — ruled out: both traffic-gen and web-frontend pods on dev-worker-1 are otherwise healthy; (5) web-frontend application crash — ruled out: both web-frontend pods remain Ready with 0 restarts.
dueDiligence.confidenceCalibration: 0.95: Starting at 1.0, reduced by -0.03 for inability to retrieve the web-frontend Service object (Knative API ambiguity), and -0.02 for not confirming whether kubelet probes for web-frontend are also affected by the NetworkPolicy (they appear exempt based on standard Kubernetes behavior). All core claims are directly evidenced.

contributingFactors: NetworkPolicy 'default-network-policy' labeled 'injected-by: kubernaut-demo' was applied 28 seconds after pod creation, cutting off previously successful connections
traffic-gen readiness probe is tightly coupled to web-frontend availability — any ingress block to web-frontend immediately marks traffic-gen as not ready

web-frontend pods appear Ready because kubelet liveness/readiness probes originate from the node (not subject to NetworkPolicy ingress rules in the same way), masking the network block from pod-to-pod traffic
The NetworkPolicy uses an empty podSelector {} which selects ALL pods in the namespace, making it a namespace-wide deny-all-ingress policy

analysis.rootCauseSummary: A NetworkPolicy 'default-network-policy' with empty podSelector and Ingress policyType but no ingress rules blocks ALL ingress traffic to every pod in demo-frontend, causing the traffic-gen readiness probe to fail when it tries to reach web-frontend:8080/healthz.

# Pod Summary — Namespace: `demo-frontend`

## Overview
3 pods found across 2 workloads. **1 pod is NOT Ready** — potential incident signal.

---

## Pod Inventory

### 1. `traffic-gen-77f9ffc59c-22cfs` ⚠️ NOT READY
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Owner** | ReplicaSet `traffic-gen-77f9ffc59c` |
| **Node** | `dev-worker-1.redhat-internal.com` |
| **Phase** | Running |
| **Ready** | ❌ **False** |
| **Container** | `curl` (curlimages/curl:8.12.1) |
| **Container Ready** | ❌ False |
| **Container Started** | ✅ Yes (started `2026-06-04T11:31:28Z`) |
| **Restart Count** | 0 |
| **QoS** | Burstable |
| **Created** | `2026-06-04T11:31:26Z` |
| **Ready Transition** | Failed at `2026-06-04T11:32:09Z` |

**Failure Reason:** `ContainersNotReady` — *"containers with unready status: [curl]"*

**What the container does:** Loops every 5s running `curl -sf http://web-frontend.demo-frontend:8080/healthz`, printing `probe=success` or `probe=failure`.

**Readiness Probe:** `curl -sf -o /dev/null -m 3 http://web-frontend.demo-frontend:8080/healthz` (initialDelay: 5s, period: 5s, timeout: 5s, failureThreshold: 3)

**Resources:** requests: 5m CPU / 16Mi RAM; limits: 20m CPU / 32Mi RAM

**Network:** OVN-Kubernetes, eth0, MAC `0a:58:0a:87:05:b3`

## Key Incident Findings

| # | Finding | Severity |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `traffic-gen` pod readiness probe is **failing** — cannot reach `web-frontend.demo-frontend:8080/healthz` | 🔴 High |
| 2 | Both `web-frontend` pods are **Ready and healthy** on port 8080 — backend appears up | ℹ️ Info |
| 3 | `traffic-gen` container is **running but not ready** (0 restarts) — probe failure is persistent, not a crash loop | 🟡 Medium |
| 4 | Both workload types land on `dev-worker-1` — possible node-level network policy or routing issue between pods on the same node | 🟡 Medium |
| 5 | All pods use OVN-Kubernetes networking with `restricted-v2` SCC | ℹ️ Info |

## Recommended Next Steps
- Check if `traffic-gen` can actually reach the `web-frontend` Service DNS (`web-frontend.demo-frontend:8080`) — possible NetworkPolicy blocking traffic
- Inspect `kubectl logs traffic-gen-77f9ffc59c-22cfs` for `probe=failure` output frequency
- Verify the `web-frontend` Service exists and has correct endpoints in `demo-frontend`
- Check for NetworkPolicy resources in `demo-frontend` namespace that may restrict ingress/egress

There's a **NetworkPolicy** called `default-network-policy` that blocks ALL ingress traffic to all pods in `demo-frontend` (empty `podSelector` + `policyTypes: [Ingress]` with no `ingress` rules). This is why the `traffic-gen` readiness probe cannot reach `web-frontend:8080/healthz`. Let me verify the web-frontend service and get more details on this NetworkPolicy.

kaResponse.analysis: NetworkPolicy 'default-network-policy' in namespace 'demo-frontend' uses an empty podSelector {} with policyTypes: [Ingress] but defines no ingress rules — the Kubernetes deny-all-ingress pattern. Applied 28 seconds after pod creation (at 11:31:54Z), it immediately cut off all pod-to-pod ingress traffic in the namespace. This caused the 'traffic-gen' container's readiness probe (HTTP GET web-frontend:8080/healthz) to fail continuously, leaving 0/1 replicas available and triggering KubeDeploymentReplicasMismatch.

rootCauseAnalysis.summary: NetworkPolicy 'default-network-policy' in namespace 'demo-frontend' uses an empty podSelector {} with policyTypes: [Ingress] but defines no ingress rules — the Kubernetes deny-all-ingress pattern. Applied 28 seconds after pod creation (at 11:31:54Z), it immediately cut off all pod-to-pod ingress traffic in the namespace. This caused the 'traffic-gen' container's readiness probe (HTTP GET web-frontend:8080/healthz) to fail continuously, leaving 0/1 replicas available and triggering KubeDeploymentReplicasMismatch.
causalChain: Signal: KubeDeploymentReplicasMismatch — Deployment 'traffic-gen' has 1 desired replica but 0 available (ready)
Why? Pod 'traffic-gen-77f9ffc59c-22cfs' is Running but its readiness probe fails persistently (25 Unhealthy events)
Why? The readiness probe 'curl -sf http://web-frontend.demo-frontend:8080/healthz' cannot reach web-frontend — returns failure
Why? NetworkPolicy 'default-network-policy' (podSelector: {}, policyTypes: [Ingress], no ingress rules) blocks ALL ingress traffic to all pods in demo-frontend, including web-frontend pods

Root cause: NetworkPolicy 'default-network-policy' implements a deny-all-ingress policy for the entire demo-frontend namespace, injected by kubernaut-demo infrastructure, with no ingress allowance rules defined

dueDiligence.targetAccuracy: Targeting NetworkPolicy/default-network-policy because it is the resource whose configuration (or deletion) directly fixes the problem. The Deployment and Pod are correctly configured — they are victims of the network policy. Adding ingress rules or deleting the NetworkPolicy would restore pod-to-pod connectivity and allow the readiness probe to pass.
dueDiligence.proportionality: The NetworkPolicy applies to the entire demo-frontend namespace (empty podSelector), affecting all pods. However, the remediation target is precisely the NetworkPolicy resource — a single, well-defined configuration change (add ingress rules or delete the policy) resolves the issue. This is appropriately scoped.
dueDiligence.scopeCompleteness: Checked: Deployment spec, ReplicaSet, Pod status/events/logs, NetworkPolicy (all policies in namespace), node assignment. Did not retrieve the Service object (Knative API ambiguity prevented it), but this does not affect the conclusion since the NetworkPolicy blocks traffic before it reaches the service. No other NetworkPolicies exist in the namespace.
dueDiligence.causalCompleteness: Traced fully: signal → pod not ready → readiness probe failure → network unreachable → NetworkPolicy deny-all-ingress. The NetworkPolicy spec was verified directly: empty podSelector + Ingress policyType + no ingress rules = deny-all. Cannot go deeper; the NetworkPolicy is the root configuration causing the block.
dueDiligence.evidenceSufficiency: All claims are backed by tool output: (1) Pod events show 25 Unhealthy readiness probe failures; (2) Pod logs show sustained probe=failure after initial successes; (3) NetworkPolicy spec confirmed: podSelector:{}, policyTypes:[Ingress], no ingress rules; (4) NetworkPolicy creationTimestamp 11:31:54Z correlates with probe failures starting at 11:31:58Z. Service object could not be retrieved (ambiguous API group with Knative), but web-frontend pods are confirmed Ready via pod listing.
dueDiligence.regressionAwareness: No prior remediation history exists for NetworkPolicy/default-network-policy in demo-frontend (confirmed via get_namespaced_resource_context). N/A — no previously failed remediations to avoid.
dueDiligence.alternativeHypotheses: Considered: (1) web-frontend service misconfiguration — ruled out: web-frontend pods are Ready and responding to kubelet probes; (2) DNS resolution failure — ruled out: pod logs show initial probe=success proving DNS and connectivity worked before the NetworkPolicy was applied; (3) Resource limits/OOMKill — ruled out: 0 restarts, container is Running; (4) Node network issues — ruled out: both traffic-gen and web-frontend pods on dev-worker-1 are otherwise healthy; (5) web-frontend application crash — ruled out: both web-frontend pods remain Ready with 0 restarts.
dueDiligence.confidenceCalibration: 0.95: Starting at 1.0, reduced by -0.03 for inability to retrieve the web-frontend Service object (Knative API ambiguity), and -0.02 for not confirming whether kubelet probes for web-frontend are also affected by the NetworkPolicy (they appear exempt based on standard Kubernetes behavior). All core claims are directly evidenced.

contributingFactors: NetworkPolicy 'default-network-policy' (labeled 'injected-by: kubernaut-demo') was applied 28 seconds after pod creation, cutting off previously successful connections
Empty podSelector {} selects ALL pods in the namespace, making it a namespace-wide deny-all-ingress policy
No ingress rules defined — every pod-to-pod ingress request is silently dropped
traffic-gen readiness probe is tightly coupled to web-frontend availability on port 8080 — any ingress block immediately marks traffic-gen as not ready
web-frontend pods appear Ready because kubelet liveness/readiness probes originate from the node and bypass NetworkPolicy enforcement, masking the network block
